elements.sent-2411-001: A kinetic model for granular mixtures is considered to study three different non-equilibrium situations.
elements.sent-2411-002: The model is based on the equivalence between a gas of elastic hard spheres subjected to a drag force proportional to the particle velocity and a gas of inelastic hard spheres.
elements.sent-2411-003: As a first problem, the relaxation of the velocity moments to their forms in the homogeneous cooling state (HCS) is studied.
elements.sent-2411-004: Then, taking the HCS as the reference state, the kinetic model is solved by the Chapman-Enskog method, which is conveniently adapted to inelastic collisions.
elements.sent-2411-005: For small spatial gradients, the mass, momentum and heat fluxes of the mixture are determined and exact expressions for the Navier-Stokes transport coefficients are obtained.
elements.sent-2411-006: As a third nonequilibrium problem, the kinetic model is solved exactly in the uniform shear flow (USF) state, where the rheological properties of the mixture are computed in terms of the parameter space of the mixture.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition to the transport properties, the velocity distribution functions of each species are also explicitly obtained.
elements.sent-2411-008: To assess the reliability of the model, its theoretical predictions are compared with both (approximate) analytical results and computer simulations of the original Boltzmann equation.
elements.sent-2411-009: In general, the comparison shows a reasonable agreement between the two kinetic equations.
elements.sent-2411-010: While the diffusion transport coefficients show excellent agreement with the Boltzmann results, more quantitative differences appear in the case of the shear viscosity coefficient and the heat flux transport coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-011: In the case of the USF, although the model qualitatively captures the shear rate dependence of the rheological properties well, the discrepancies increase with increasing inelasticity in collisions.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is now well established that granular media behave like a fluid when externally excited.
elements.sent-2411-013: Under these conditions (rapid flow conditions), granular media can be modeled as a gas of hard spheres with inelastic collisions.
elements.sent-2411-014: In the simplest version, the spheres are assumed to be completely smooth and so the inelasticity is accounted for by a (constant) positive coefficient of normal restitution.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the low-density regime, the Boltzmann equation (conveniently generalized to dissipative dynamics) has been used as a starting point to derive the corresponding Navier-Stokes hydrodynamic equations with explicit forms for the transport coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-016: These equations are usually solved by considering the leading terms in a Sonine polynomial expansion.
elements.sent-2411-017: This procedure becomes more tedious in the case of granular mixtures, since not only are the number of transport coefficients greater than for a single component gas, but they also depend on more parameters.
elements.sent-2411-018: For this reason, it is therefore quite common in kinetic theory to resort to alternative approaches for such far from equilibrium states.
elements.sent-2411-019: One possibility is to keep the structure of the (inelastic) Boltzmann collision operator but to assume a different interaction model: the so-called inelastic Maxwell model (IMM).
elements.sent-2411-020: As for the conventional Maxwell molecules, \cite{CC70 IMM's are characterized by the property that the collision rate is independent of the relative velocity of the two colliding spheres.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Another possible alternative for obtaining accurate results is to consider a kinetic model equation of the inelastic Boltzmann equation for hard spheres.
elements.sent-2411-022: Kinetic models have proven to be very useful for the analysis of transport properties in far-from-equilibrium states of dilute molecular gases.
elements.sent-2411-023: In fact, for several non-equilibrium situations, exact solutions of the kinetic models have been shown to agree very well with Monte Carlo simulations of the Boltzmann equation for molecular gases.
elements.sent-2411-024: In fact, we are aware of only one kinetic model proposed years ago by Vega Reyes et al. \cite{VGS07 This is in contrast to the large number of kinetic models proposed in the literature for molecular mixtures.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Here, for the sake of simplicity, we have adopted the Gross and Krook (GK) model \cite{GK56 proposed many years ago for studying transport properties in multicomponent molecular gases.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Thus, the kinetic model employed in this paper can be considered as a direct extension of the GK model to granular mixtures.
elements.sent-2411-027: Although the kinetic model of Vega Reyes et al. \cite{VGS07 was reported several years ago, to the best of our knowledge it has not been considered so far to study linear and nonlinear transport properties of granular mixtures.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The aim of this paper is to consider the above kinetic model to determine the dynamical properties of granular binary mixtures in different non-equilibrium situations.
elements.sent-2411-029: In addition, apart from obtaining the above properties, the simplicity of the model allows one to get the explicit forms of the velocity distribution functions.
elements.sent-2411-030: This is likely one of the main advantages of using a kinetic model instead of the original Boltzmann equation.
elements.sent-2411-031: Three different but related problems are studied.
elements.sent-2411-032: First, the so-called HCS is analyzed; we are mainly interested here in studying the relaxation of the velocity moments towards their HCS expressions (starting from arbitrary initial conditions).
elements.sent-2411-033: Then, once the HCS is well characterized for the mixture, we solve the kinetic model using the Chapman-Enskog method \cite{CC70 for states close to the HCS.}
elements.sent-2411-034: In contrast to the results obtained from the Boltzmann equation, \cite{GD02,SGNT06,GMD06,SNG07,GM07 exact expressions for the complete set of Navier-Stokes transport coefficients of the mixture are derived in terms of the parameter space of the system.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Finally, as a third problem, the rheological properties of a binary granular mixture USF are obtained explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-036: The search for exact solutions of kinetic models is interesting not only from a formal point of view, but also as a way to assess the reliability of these solutions.
elements.sent-2411-037: To gauge their accuracy, we compare in this paper the theoretical predictions of the kinetic model with (i) (approximate) analytical results of the original Boltzmann equation and with (ii) computer simulation results available in the granular literature.
elements.sent-2411-038: This type of comparison allows us to measure the degree of reliability of the kinetic model for describing granular flows under realistic conditions.
elements.sent-2411-039: The structure of the paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-040: In Sec.\ \ref{sec2 we introduce the original Boltzmann equation for granular mixtures and its balance hydrodynamic equations, and present the explicit form of the kinetic model.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section \ref{sec3 deals with the HCS: a homogeneous state with a granular temperature decaying with time.}
elements.sent-2411-042: As said before, we first study the relaxation of the velocity moments to their (steady) asymptotic expressions.
elements.sent-2411-043: It is shown that for certain values of the parameters of the system, quite high velocity moments can diverge in time in the HCS.
elements.sent-2411-044: Section \ref{sec4 is devoted to the application of the Chapman-Enskog method to the kinetic model for obtaining the Navier-Stokes transport coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Their expressions are also compared with both theoretical approximations and computer simulations obtained from the Boltzmann equation.
elements.sent-2411-046: The USF is studied in Sec.\ \ref{sec5 while a brief discussion of the results reported in the paper is given in Sec.\ \ref{sec6 .}}
elements.section-2411-002: Boltzmann kinetic equation for granular mixtures. A kinetic model
elements.sent-2411-047: We consider an isolated binary granular mixture of inelastic hard spheres of masses $m_i$ and diameters $\sigma_i$ ($i=1,2$).
elements.sent-2411-048: The subscript $i$ labels one of the $s$ mechanically different species or components of the mixture.
elements.sent-2411-049: We assume also for simplicity that the spheres are completely smooth and hence, in a binary collision of particles of the species $i$ with particles of the species $j$ while the magnitude of the tangential component of the relative velocity of the two colliding spheres remains unaltered, its normal component is reversed and shrunk by a factor $\al_{ij}$.
elements.sent-2411-050: The parameter $\al_{ij}$ ($0<\al_{ij}\leqslant 1$) is called the (constant) coefficient of normal restitution and accounts for the energy dissipated in each binary collision between particles of species $i$ and $j$.
elements.sent-2411-051: In the low-density limit, a kinetic theory description is appropriate, and the one-particle velocity distribution function $f_i(\mathbf{r , \mathbf{v , t)$ of species $i$ verifies the set of two-coupled nonlinear integro-differential Boltzmann kinetic equations \cite{G19 \beq \frac{\partial {\partial t f_i+\mathbf{v}\cdot \nabla f_i=\sum_{j=1 ^2 J_{ij [\mathbf{v |f_i,f_j], \quad i=1,2.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Its explicit form can be found for instance in Ref.\ \onlinecite{G19 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: At a hydrodynamic level, the relevant fields are the number densities $n_i$, the flow velocity $\mathbf{U}$, and the granular temperature $T$.
elements.sent-2411-054: In terms of moments of the velocity distribution functions $f_i$, they are defined as \beq n_i=\int d\mathbf{v f_i(\mathbf{v ), \eeq \beq \rho \mathbf{U =\sum_{i ^2\; m_i n_i \mathbf{U _i=\sum_{i=1 ^2\;m_i \int d\mathbf{v \mathbf{v f_i(\mathbf{v ), \eeq \beq n T=p=\sum_{i ^2\; n_i T_i =\sum_{i=1 ^2\;\frac{m_i {3 \int d\mathbf{v \mathbf{V ^2f_i(\mathbf{v ).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Furthermore, the second equality in Eq.\ \eqref{2.3 and the third equality in Eq.\ \eqref{2.4 define the flow velocity $\mathbf{U _i$ and the kinetic temperature $T_i$ for species $i$, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The partial temperature $T_i$ is a measure of the mean kinetic of energy of particles of species $i$.
elements.sent-2411-057: The Boltzmann collision operators $J_{ij [f_i,f_j]$ conserve the number density of each species and the total momentum in each collision $ij$: \beq \int d\mathbf{v J_{ij [\mathbf{v |f_i,f_j]=0, \eeq \beq m_i\int d\mathbf{v \mathbf{v J_{ij [\mathbf{v |f_i,f_j]+m_j\int d\mathbf{v \mathbf{v J_{ji [\mathbf{v |f_j,f_i]=0.}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-067: As mentioned in Sec.\ \ref{sec1 , the idea behind the construction of a kinetic model is to replace the operator $J_{ij [f_i,f_j]$ for IHS by a simpler mathematical collision term that retains its relevant physical properties.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: While in the case of molecular mixtures many different kinetic models have been proposed in the literature, \cite{GK56,S62,H65,H66,GS67,GSB89,AAP02,HHKPW21 kinetic models for granular mixtures are much more scarce.}

elements.sent-2411-236: The Chapman-Enskog solution of the inelastic Boltzmann equation for states with small spatial gradients is technically difficult but accessible.
elements.sent-2411-237: For more complex far from equilibrium states, the Boltzmann equation for granular mixtures becomes intractable.
elements.sent-2411-238: In these cases, kinetic models are used as a reliable alternative.
elements.sent-2411-239: Here, as a third problem in the paper, we study in this section the so-called simple or uniform shear flow (USF) state.
elements.sent-2411-240: Although this state has been extensively studied in the case of single component granular gases,\cite{LSJCh84,JR88,C89,HS92,LB94,GT96,SGN96,GS96,BRM97,ChR98,MGSB99,SGD04 the studies for granular mixtures are more scarce.}
elements.sent-2411-241: At the microscopic level, one of the main advantages of the USF over other non-equilibrium problems is that in this state the spatial dependence of the distribution functions $f_i(\mathbf{r , \mathbf{v ; t)$ arises only from their dependence on the peculiar velocity $V_k = v_k - a_{k \ell r_\ell$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: Thus, when the particle velocities are expressed in the Lagrangian frame moving with the flow velocity $U_k = a_{k \ell r_\ell$, the USF becomes spatially homogeneous.}
elements.sent-2411-244: This property is probably the main reason why this state has been studied extensively in molecular and granular gases, as it provides a clear framework for studying the nonlinear response of the system to strong shear.
elements.sent-2411-245: However, the nature of the USF state is quite different for molecular and granular fluids, since in the latter a steady state is possible (without introducing external thermostats) when the viscous heating term is exactly compensated by the energy dissipated by collisions.
elements.sent-2411-246: Here we are mainly interested in obtaining the non-Newtonian transport properties of the mixture under steady conditions.
elements.sent-2411-247: In the steady state ($\partial_t f_i=0$), the set of kinetic equations \eqref{2.22 for the model reads \beq -2a V_y \frac{\partial f_1 {\partial V_x +\omega_1 f_1-\epsilon_{1 \frac{\partial {\partial {\bf V }\cdot (\mathbf{V f_1)=\Phi_1, \eeq \beq -2a V_y \frac{\partial f_2 {\partial V_x +\omega_2 f_2-\epsilon_{2 \frac{\partial {\partial {\bf V }\cdot (\mathbf{V f_2)=\Phi_2.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-248: As a consequence, the relevant balance equation in the USF is that of the granular temperature $T$, Eq.\ \eqref{2.11 .}
elements.sent-2411-249: In the steady state, Eq.\ \eqref{2.11 becomes \beq \frac{2 {3 n a P_{xy +\zeta T=0, \eeq where the pressure tensor $P_{k \ell}$ is defined by Eq.\ \eqref{2.13 and the expression of the cooling rate in the kinetic model is given by Eq.\ \eqref{2.8 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-250: As mentioned earlier, there are two competing effects in the granular temperature equation according to Eq.\ \eqref{4.3 .}
elements.sent-2411-251: On the one hand, the viscous heating term ($aP_{xy <0$) causes the granular temperature to increase monotonically with time.}
elements.sent-2411-252: On the other hand, since collisions are inelastic, there is a continuous loss of energy due to the collisional cooling term ($\zeta T>0$).

elements.sent-2411-284: However, in contrast to the HCS results, it is quite obvious that the effect of inelasticity on the shape of the velocity distribution function is less pronounced in the USF than in the HCS.

elements.sent-2411-288: The determination of transport properties of multicomponent mixtures from the Boltzmann equation is in general a rather complicated problem.
elements.sent-2411-289: Because of these technical difficulties, researchers have usually considered kinetic model equations in which the Boltzmann collision operators $J_{ij [f_i,f_j]$ are replaced by terms that retain the relevant physical properties of these operators but are mathematically simpler.}
elements.sent-2411-290: This procedure has been widely used in the past years in the case of molecular mixtures, where several models \cite{GK56,S62,H65,H66,GS67,GSB89,AAP02,HHKPW21 have been proposed to obtain explicit expressions for the transport coefficients of the mixture.}
elements.sent-2411-291: However, much fewer models have been proposed for granular mixtures (mixtures of mechanically different hard spheres undergoing inelastic collisions).
elements.sent-2411-292: In fact, as mentioned in Sec.\ \ref{sec1 , we are only aware of the kinetic model proposed by Vega Reyes et al. \cite{VGS07 for a mixture of inelastic hard spheres.}}
elements.sent-2411-293: This model is inspired in the equivalence between a gas of elastic hard spheres subject to a drag force with a gas of IHS.
elements.sent-2411-294: Three different non-equilibrium situations were considered.
elements.sent-2411-295: As a first step, the HCS was analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-296: The study of this state is crucial for the determination of the Navier-Stokes transport coefficients of the mixture, since the local version of the HCS plays the role of the reference state in the Chapman-Enskog perturbative method.
elements.sent-2411-297: This kind of divergence could question the validity of a normal (or hydrodynamic) solution of the Boltzmann equation in the HCS.
elements.sent-2411-298: Once the HCS is well characterized, as a second problem we have obtained the exact forms of the Navier-Stokes transport coefficients in terms of the parameter space of the system.
elements.sent-2411-299: Finally, as a third problem, the rheological properties of a sheared granular mixture have also been derived.
elements.sent-2411-300: It is important to note that the use of the kinetic model has allowed not only to obtain the exact forms of the linear and nonlinear transport properties of the mixture, but also to obtain the explicit forms of the velocity distribution functions.
elements.sent-2411-301: This is one of the main advantages of considering a kinetic model of the Boltzmann equation.
elements.sent-2411-302: Comparison with both the (approximate) theoretical results of the Boltzmann equation and computer simulations shows in some cases an excellent agreement (temperature ratio in the HCS and the diffusion transport coefficients), in others a reasonable quantitative agreement (the Navier--Sokes shear viscosity and the rheological properties), while more significant discrepancies are present in the case of the heat flux transport coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-303: Regarding the velocity distribution functions, based on previous comparisons with DSMC results, \cite{BRM97 it is expected that the model gives accurate results for small velocities, but important differences are likely to appear in the high-velocity region.}
elements.sent-2411-304: We hope that the present paper will stimulate the performance of these simulations to confirm the above expectations.
elements.sent-2411-305: In conclusion, the results reported here can be considered as a testimony of the reliability of the kinetic model \eqref{2.22 for the study of nonequilibrium problems where the use of the original Boltzmann equation turns out to be unapproachable.}
elements.sent-2411-306: In particular, the derivation of the transport coefficients of a granular binary mixture characterizing the transport around the USF is an interesting project for the near future.
elements.sent-2411-307: Given the technical difficulties involved in such a calculation, the kinetic model \eqref{2.22 can be considered as a useful starting point.}
